Alright, welcome back. Let's dive right in. Now here's the deal. I created olfactory anchoring almost a decade ago because what was happening in my own life, short story is, you know, I'm capable of telling a short story, I'll do my best here, I promise you, right? Anyway, I was on my first global speaking tour down in Australia and I was finding myself in these intense situations where it was absolutely crucial for me to be in an empowered state, despite the fact that everything around me was just like, hey, why I was working heavily against me? For example, I had this manager, a great guy, I love the guy, right? He's trying to build my brand, but he kept cornering me into these last second TV interviews, talk show requests, radio shows, loose paint interviews, you get it? Photo ops? I mean, you name it, I was doing it and all of them had to look my best, act my best and actually be my best, no matter how tired I was or jet lag there was or burnt out, I mean, I just started my tour, just getting used to the six month tour I was on, right? And I had to perform every day at the highest level, kind of pain in the butt, but that was the reality I was facing. Now at the time, I just started using a state management technique called NLP anchoring, which you may have heard of, but either way, it's part of a group strategies that make up a body of psychology known as neuro-linguistic programming or NLP for short. Now, just to give you a bit of insight here, the basic premise of NLP anchoring is that as human beings, we have the ability to choose how we feel at any particular moment in time, as opposed to being chosen or dictated to us, but what's going on in the surrounding environment or our personal lives. In other words, to NLP's way of thinking, we human beings need to be proactive when it comes to choosing our emotional state as opposed to us being reactive, meaning we react what's going on around us, which is how most of us have been conditioned to think since we were kids, right? That, you know, what's going on around us is what makes us feel a certain way. Now, if you think this through for a second, it's obvious that NLP's proactive take on state management, well, it's pretty attractive. In fact, it's very attractive to anyone in sales and to that, that any success-oriented person who wants to live a very empowered life. I mean, after all, the ability to manage your emotional state, meaning to be able to trigger a positive state, those key moments in life when it matters most, it has obvious huge advantages, right? I mean, of course it does. If for no other reason why I stated in the introduction, it's very obvious. So with that in mind, let me quickly explain the philosophy behind NLP anchoring, which is based on two simple truths or great truths. The first one, having to do with our God-given ability or nature, if that's what you believe, right? Either way, it's our ability as human beings to actually choose what we want to focus on. In other words, at any particular moment in time, you have the ability to choose the direction of your focus. And then based on that choice, you'll tend to fall into a state that's basically congruent with what you're focused on. For example, if you spend the next five minutes focusing everything that's right in your life, everything that's great in your life, a recent business success, being in a great relationship with a partner, healthy children, a recent goal you achieved, right? Focusing on all that stuff, well, then you'll tend to quickly pop into a positive, empowered state that reflects all those wonderful things, right? Makes perfect sense. And now, conversely, if you spend the same amount of time focusing everything that's wrong in your life, a recent business failure, a divorce, like me, right? A sick child got a bit, or a recent goal that you didn't achieve, then you'll tend to fall into a very disempowered state that reflects all those crappy things, right? As simple as that. So that's that first great truth of NLP anchoring, that as human beings, we get to choose the direction of our focus and then the result of that decision, we tend to fall into a state that's congruent with that choice, bottom line. And now second, we also have control of the way we move or hold our body, which in scientific terms is called your physiology. Essentially, physiology is just the fan theory of saying all the possible ways you can hold or move your body at any given moment in time. For example, your posture, your facial expressions, how you move your appendages, your rate of breathing even, your overall level of motion. And just so you know, interesting point here, the way physiology works in terms of how it affects someone's emotional state, it's identical across the entire world and in every culture. In other words, as a species, we all adapt the same physiology when we're in a positive emotional state. And also the same will be it in a different physiology when we're in a negative disempowered state. In other words, people who are born in Papua New Guinea don't smile when they're depressed or frown when they're happy any more than an Eskimo would or a person from Portugal or you would. For example, if I pointed to a closed door and said to you behind that door is a very happy woman. For a prize of $10,000, is this happy woman smiling or is she frowning? Now think this through here, the happy woman, does she smile or does she frown? Absolutely, let's try another one. Okay, well, I'm going to assume that you weren't really paying attention here because I think it's pretty obvious to all of us humans, let's just from Mars, right, that we tend not to frown or happy, we smile. Let's try another one. Is her head held high or is her head slumped over? Which one makes more sense to you? Bingo, you got it. Let's try another one. Really? You screwed me here. Come on. Let's try one more. Are our shoulders pulled back or are they slightly roll forward? Which one of those two makes more sense to you for this happy woman? Of course, they're pulled back. Let's do one more. Rolled slightly forward. Wow. Okay, well, I guess you don't know that much about body language and you don't want that tank ran. Let's try one more. Are her eyes open wide or are they slightly narrowed? Which one makes more sense for this happy woman? Open wide, you bet, you got it. Eyes slightly narrowed means happy to you. Okay, well, I guess you didn't really want that $10,000, now did you? Now, just so you know, I've asked both of these versions of all these questions to live audiences all over the world, UK, Australia, South Africa, China, Russia, Singapore, Mexico, Malaysia, Canada, Iceland, you name it. I've asked this question everywhere and here's the deal. No matter where I ask it, everyone always answers these questions the exact same way. The bottom line is that we all know what a depressed person looks like. Some of us too well. I hate to say, right? We also know what a happy person looks like. We also know what an angry person looks like and we know what a loving person looks like. We know what an impatient person looks like. We know what a calm person looks like. It's all very logical. Now, let me give you another state matter of question here. One more, right? It's very logical. Now, here's the deal. We're slightly different angle. Let's say you were in a depressed state right now and I offered you $100,000 to act happy for the next 45 seconds. Could you do that? In other words, could you act happy even if you really felt sad inside of it? I offered you $100,000 for 45 seconds. You get it? Could you do that? Yes, of course you could. It wouldn't be that difficult at all. Really, you could not happy for 45 seconds for $100,000. I mean, think about it. You've got to be just messing with me here because I'm sure you actually could. It would simply be a matter of consciously making certain obvious changes to your physiology, ones that you've used countless times throughout your life since you were basically old enough to walk. You get it? But let me ask you another question now. What if I offered you that same, no, let's make it even $200,000, a quarter million bucks, right? And I asked you to make some changes to your physiology like the last time, right? Since you're feeling terrible, but I say you got to act happy. Except this time, I asked you to maintain that artificial happy physiology for the next 18 hours straight. Could you do that? In other words, could you act happy for 18 hours straight? You're absolutely right. There's no possible way. No matter how hard you try, you simply couldn't do it. It's impossible. Wow, I respect your confidence, but I got to play something physiologically. It's impossible. No matter how hard you try, you simply couldn't pull it off. Too difficult. Now, just to be clear, this has nothing to do with you being weak or strong or anything of the sort. It's simply not how human beings are built. See, well, it's true we can make certain changes in the moment, but that moment is fleeting. So we can feel a certain way, but it's fleeting. It's going to pass after maybe five or six minutes, maybe an hour at most. After that, bottom line, you're going to slowly start to settle back into whatever state you were naturally in before you tried to change it. Get it? Now, one more point here. If any of this seems far into you or confusing, just think back to all the times in your life when someone asked you to manage your state and you actually did it, but you called it something else. For example, how many times has someone said to you, keep your chin up or put on a brave face or look alert, well, keep cool, smile and act polite. Don't lose your temper. You get it? The realities that we've all tried to manage our state at various, in fact, no many points in our life and many times we were successful, but many times we weren't. The goal of NLP anchoring is to eliminate the warrant successful bottom line. You get it? So with that, let's stop right now. I want you to take a quick quiz and then please come right back. Let's plow forward because you're going to love what comes next. I'll see you soon.
